Alternatively, I can look up the standard DoD banner. The typical DoD banner is:

"You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions:

- The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (e.g., discrimination, sexual harassment, hostile work environment), and law enforcement (e.g., employee theft, child pornography, espionage, terrorism).

- Notwithstanding the above, the USG may conduct intercept and monitor communications on this IS for any other purpose.

- At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS.

- Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, and are protected by the Fourth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.

- This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests--not for your personal benefit or privacy.

- You have no reasonable expectation of privacy regarding any communication or data transiting or stored on this IS.

- At any time, and for any lawful government purpose, the USG may monitor, intercept, search, and seize data transiting or stored on this IS.

- Use of this IS constitutes consent to the following:	You will not use this IS for personal purposes. You will not use this IS to conduct any activity that is unlawful or contrary to USG policy. You will not use this IS to threaten, intimidate, harass, or otherwise discriminate against anyone because of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, or genetic information.

- Use of this IS for personal purposes is prohibited. Use of this IS for unofficial purposes is prohibited.

- The USG may conduct intercept and monitor communications on this IS for any other purpose.

- Use of this IS constitutes consent to the following:	By using this IS, you expressly consent to the monitoring, interception, search, and seizure of data transiting or stored on this IS for any lawful government purpose.

- Notwithstanding the above, the USG may conduct intercept and monitor communications on this IS for any other purpose.

- At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS.

1. Read the current /etc/issue file to verify its existence and content.
2. Write the complete Department of Defense (DoD) required login banner text to /etc/issue. The text will be:

You will not use this IS to threaten, intimidate, harass, or otherwise discriminate against anyone because of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, or genetic information.
   - Use of this IS for personal purposes is prohibited. Use of this IS for unofficial purposes is prohibited.

   I've read and I consent to these terms and conditions."

   This text includes all required paragraphs and the short consent option.

3. Set file permissions to 0644 (readable by all, writable only by root) using chmod.

4. Verify by reading the file back.

I'll use the write_file tool to write the content. Since write_file takes a path and content, I'll provide the content as a multi-line string. In the tool call, I need to pass the content as a string with newline characters. I can represent newlines as \n or actually include them. line2
..." > /etc/issue

But I'd need to pass the entire command as a string. That's fine.

But using write_file might be simpler: I can call write_file with the content as a string that includes newline characters. In the JSON, I can include \n for newlines, and the tool will interpret them as actual newlines when writing. I think that's acceptable.

Given the instructions: "Use run_cmd for EXACTLY ONE shell command at a time." So if I use write_file, that's a single tool call, not a shell command. That's fine. The tool write_file will write the content to the file. So I can use write_file.

Thus, the plan will involve:
- read_file("/etc/issue") to inspect.
- write_file("/etc/issue", content, mode="0644") to write.
- maybe run_cmd("cat /etc/issue") to verify, but that's optional.
